text:
Radioimmunotherapy (RIT) is an increasingly promising strategy for cancer treatment, yet its efficacy remains hampered by poor tumor penetration and suboptimal pharmacokinetics of traditional targeting agents. While small radiolabeled antibody fragments and peptides offer high specificity and minimal accumulation in healthy tissues, they frequently suffer from high renal uptake. Nanobodies—the smallest functional antigen-binding fragments derived from camelid heavy-chain antibodies—face similar challenges. In this study, we demonstrate that renal retention of nanobodies is primarily driven by the number of polar residues in their C-terminal amino acid tags.

We evaluated three nanobody variants with different C-terminal configurations: Myc-His-tagged, His-tagged, and untagged. Dynamic planar imaging in Wistar rats using 111In-DTPA-labeled nanobodies showed that untagged variants achieved a 70% reduction in kidney accumulation compared to Myc-His-tagged versions at 50 minutes post-injection. When untagged nanobodies were co-infused with the plasma expander Gelofusin, renal uptake dropped by 90%. Consistent results were observed in HER2-positive tumor-xenografted mice using 177Lu-DTPA-2Rs15d nanobodies; the combination of the untagged construct and Gelofusin co-infusion reduced kidney accumulation by 95% without compromising tumor targeting.

Dosimetry calculations for the untagged 177Lu-DTPA-2Rs15d with Gelofusin revealed an equivalent dose (0.90 Gy/MBq) delivered to both the tumor and the kidneys, with exceptionally low exposure to other healthy organs. In comparison, while 177Lu-DTPA-Trastuzumab delivered six times more radiation to the tumor, it also imposed a significantly higher radiation burden on the lungs, liver, spleen, bone, and blood (by factors ranging from 26 to 4180). Crucially, nanobody-based RIT in mice with established HER2-positive tumors resulted in nearly complete tumor growth inhibition and a significant improvement in event-free survival (P < 0.0001). Histological analysis confirmed the absence of renal inflammation, apoptosis, or necrosis. 

Our findings underscore the critical role of the C-terminal amino acid composition in modulating renal retention. By optimizing these sequences, we have established a robust method to enhance the therapeutic index of radiolabeled nanobodies, positioning them as powerful candidates for the treatment of metastatic and minimal residual disease.
